title: Website Design and Optimization
body:
## To achieve results online, it is essential that you keep the following considerations in mind:

-   **Content**: Your website must contain relevant information that is organized effectively.
-   **Writing**: The writing and presentation of the content must be tailored to the Internet, as it differs from other media such as print or TV.
-   **Speed**: Pages must load quickly.
-   **Platform independence**: Pages must display properly on any monitor, resolution, operating system, or browser.
-   **Usability**: the pages will be easy to use and navigate, allowing users to find information quickly.
-   **Visual presentation**: the visual presentation will be appealing and professional.
-   **Ease of discovery**: the page will be easy for visitors to find on various search engines such as Altavista and Infoseek.
-   **Interaction and Marketing**: visitors will be able to interact with the information provided, and the page will serve as a promotional and marketing tool.

Proper management of these elements is what drives results for your web pages. Below are some interesting insights you won’t find just anywhere on this exciting topic!

### Platform Independence

Make sure your pages display well and without any issues on any browser, monitor, resolution, or operating system. Imagine if the Super Bowl could only be watched on 31-inch Hitachi televisions. Can you imagine how small a percentage of the population would be able to watch the event? Obviously, it would have very few advertisers and viewers and wouldn’t be the huge business it is today. Now think of someone telling you: “This page must be viewed with Netscape 12.50 or Internet Explorer 66.6, or at a specific resolution, or from 50 meters away from the monitor.” Pages must load quickly Design your pages to load quickly and convey their message as fast as possible. Many sites rely on graphics and frames for their homepages, and all they achieve is making visitors frustrated and causing them to leave. Try to include only the necessary images, without making the design dependent on them. We recommend NOT using frames (two reasons not to use frames are that visitors cannot add them to their bookmarks or favorites, and that documents in frames are not indexed correctly by search engines).

### Usability

A website is usable to the extent that it contains valuable information, is fast, and allows users to easily find the information they are looking for and navigate the site intuitively, among other things. Usability is one of the tools LEAST used in web design, when it should perhaps be the most important. When we create a website, we are creating a user interface for thousands or millions of potential visitors. The least we can do for them—and for ourselves—is to make our site easy to use.

### Effective Writing

People DO NOT read when browsing the web. Nor do they like to scroll through a page, unless they’re really interested. (I should take this opportunity, by the way, to thank you for reading this far. Thank you!) The main content and conclusions should be presented right from the start, using the writing model known as the inverted pyramid—widely used in journalism. The text should be organized using headings and subheadings, and visual aids such as highlighted text, bold, and italics should be provided to help visitors navigate the page comfortably. Links are visual aids in and of themselves. Often, the only thing users read on web pages are the links, so they must be planned carefully.

### Content

The most important aspect of your web pages is the content and information you include that is useful to your visitors. The most common mistake made when presenting content is the tendency to talk about ourselves and our own processes. It is very common to create a design that pleases company executives, when the people we should be pleasing are our visitors. Another common mistake is presenting vital information in graphics without providing alternative text.
